title: Estimating Techniques
summary: How projects estimate cost and duration and how those estimates tighten as work is progressively elaborated. Reorganised into scan-friendly blocks and framed for PMBOK 8's Planning and Measurement performance domains, with predictive techniques, PERT, and agile relative sizing side by side.

sections.heading: Where estimating lives
sections.body: Estimating is not its own knowledge area in PMBOK 8. It supports two performance domains: Planning (organise the work in a deliberate, coordinated way) and Measurement (produce reliable data for timely decisions). Tailor the technique to how much you know and to the delivery approach.

blocks.title: How to use estimates
lines: Planning domain: estimates size and sequence the work.
Measurement domain: estimates become the baseline you measure against.
Tailoring: pick the technique that fits the information and the delivery mode.
Predictive leans on cost/duration estimates; agile leans on relative sizing.

sections.heading: Estimate ranges and the cone of uncertainty
sections.body: An estimate is a range, not a single number. Early on you know little, so the range is wide; as detail emerges (progressive elaboration), the range narrows and accuracy improves. Always express an estimate with its range and assumptions, and refine it as better information arrives rather than treating the first figure as fixed.

rows.value: Roughly -25% to +75%. Used very early when information is scarce.
Roughly -5% to +10%. Produced later once scope and detail are well understood.

blocks.title: Trap
lines: A number with no range is not an estimate.
ROM is wide (-25 / +75); definitive is tight (-5 / +10).
Range narrows as you move later in the project with more detail.

sections.heading: Methods, when to use, accuracy
sections.body: Match the technique to how much you know. Analogous early, parametric when data is sound, bottom-up when scope is defined and accuracy matters, three-point when there is uncertainty to model.

rows.value: Adjust a similar past project's actuals. Use early with little detail. Fast and cheap but least accurate; a form of expert judgment.
Rate x quantity from a statistical relationship (e.g. cost/sq m x area). Use when historical data is sound and scales. Accuracy depends entirely on the data and model.
Estimate each work package on the WBS, then roll up. Use when scope is well defined. Most accurate but most time- and effort-intensive; needs a defined WBS first.
Risk-weighted average of optimistic, most likely and pessimistic. Use when activities are uncertain. Improves on a single-point guess by modelling the spread.

groups.label: How
lines: Take a similar past project's actual cost/duration.
Adjust for known differences in size, scope or complexity.

groups.label: Trade-off
lines: Fast, cheap, rough.
Assumes the past project is genuinely comparable.

groups.label: How
lines: Multiply a known rate by a quantity.
Example: hours per unit x number of units.

groups.label: Trade-off
lines: More accurate than analogous when data is good.
Only as good as the historical data behind it.

groups.label: How
lines: Decompose to work-package level of the WBS.
Estimate each piece, then roll up to a total.

groups.label: Trade-off
lines: Most accurate; nothing is averaged away.
Most costly; requires a defined scope and WBS.

blocks.title: Cues
lines: "Similar past project" -> analogous.
"Cost per unit" or "rate x quantity" -> parametric.
"Estimate each work package" or "per the WBS" -> bottom-up.
"Optimistic / most likely / pessimistic" -> three-point (PERT).

sections.heading: PERT formulas
sections.body: Three-point estimating models uncertainty by combining three values. The beta (PERT) form weights the most-likely estimate by four.

blocks.title: PERT
blocks.note: O = optimistic, M = most likely, P = pessimistic

lines: Expected estimate (beta/PERT).
Most-likely value weighted by 4.
Estimate spread.
Optimistic and pessimistic set the range.

blocks.title: PERT tip
lines: Weight the most-likely value by 4, then divide by 6.
A wider O-to-P gap means a larger standard deviation and more risk.
Use PERT when a single number would hide real uncertainty.

sections.heading: Relative estimating
sections.body: Adaptive teams estimate relative size and effort rather than absolute cost or duration, then let velocity turn size into a forecast. This fits the Planning domain when requirements change and detail emerges each iteration.

items.text: Relative units of size/effort/complexity, not hours. Often a Fibonacci-like scale (1, 2, 3, 5, 8).
Team reveals point estimates simultaneously, discusses outliers, and re-votes to reach consensus.
Group many items quickly by grouping similar-sized stories together (e.g. small / medium / large buckets).
Points completed per iteration; used to forecast how much fits in future iterations.

blocks.title: Agile tip
lines: Points are relative, not hours; don't convert them to time.
Planning poker surfaces hidden assumptions through discussion.
Affinity sizing is fastest for large backlogs.
Velocity x iterations gives the forecast, not a fixed commitment.

sections.heading: Choosing, refining and reserves
sections.body: Pick the technique by the information available; estimates then feed the baseline. Reserves sit on top of your estimates, not inside them.

items.title: Analogous early
lines: When there is little information to work with.

items.title: Parametric next
lines: When you have good historical data and reliable parameters.

items.title: Bottom-up when defined
lines: When scope and the WBS are set and accuracy matters most.

items.title: Add three-point for risk
lines: When activities carry real uncertainty to model.

rows.value: For identified 'known-unknown' risks. Part of the cost baseline. Detail in the Risk Management guide.
For 'unknown-unknowns'. Outside the cost baseline; needs approval to use.

blocks.title: Wrap-up
lines: Pick the technique by the information available.
Estimates feed the baseline; reserves are added on top, not buried inside.
Progressively refine rather than treating the first figure as final.

source: PM Cram study notes — restructured into blocks and aligned to PMBOK 8 Planning and Measurement performance domains.
